title: Oligarchy’s Ancient Origins
gist: Historian Matt Simonton traces how classical Greek oligarchies used deliberately engineered institutions — property qualifications, co-optation, informant rewards, and external military backers like Sparta — to preserve minority rule against unpopular odds, then argues that the modern US Senate filibuster performs the same anti-majoritarian function the Federalists themselves warned against, making America 'effectively governed by an oligarchy' today.

themes.theme: Democracy vs. oligarchy in classical Greece

insights.claim: In 403 BCE a democratic resistance movement in Athens, including working-class resident foreigners and slaves, overthrew the authoritarian regime known as the Thirty after nearly a year of civil war; the Thirty had executed more than 1,500 people despite claiming to rule with 'virtue' and 'justice.'
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Establishes the historical baseline case study — a violent oligarchic regime overthrown by democratic restoration — that frames the essay's argument about how oligarchies seize and lose power.
insights.locator: In the autumn of 403 BCE, democracy triumphed in Athens

insights.claim: Critias — an intellectual, poet, and political theorist related to Plato who led the anti-democratic Thirty until his death in battle — reportedly had a marble gravestone erected by fellow partisans depicting Oligarchy personified setting Democracy on fire, with an epitaph honoring 'good men, who for a brief time restrained the hubris of the cursed Athenian people'; some scholars doubt the monument's existence since it is known only from a manuscript marginal note.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Illustrates the ideological self-conception of ancient oligarchs as a virtuous elite justifiably suppressing the majority — a rhetorical template Simonton implicitly links to modern elite self-justifications for minority rule.
insights.quote: This is the memorial of good men, who for a brief time restrained the hubris of the cursed Athenian people
insights.locator: To pay tribute to Critias and the entire anti-democratic movement

insights.claim: Ancient Greek thinkers classified constitutions by the number of rulers — the one (tyranny), the few (oligarchy), or the many (democracy) — and Aristotle observed in The Politics that most Greek constitutions were either democratic or oligarchic, making oligarchy, not tyranny, democracy's chief historical rival.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Provides the classical framework the whole essay is built on — that the central political conflict in Greek antiquity, as in Simonton's reading of America today, was democracy vs. oligarchy, not democracy vs. tyranny.
insights.quote: most constitutions are either democratic or oligarchic
insights.locator: Writing in the fourth century BCE, Aristotle observed

insights.claim: Columbia historian John Ma describes the period roughly 460–360 BCE as a 'Hundred Years' War' between democracy and oligarchy in the Greek world.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Quantifies the duration and intensity of the ancient democracy-oligarchy conflict cited as precedent for the essay's modern argument.
insights.quote: refers to the period between about 460 and 360 BCE as a “Hundred Years’ War” between democracy and oligarchy

insights.claim: Greek oligarchies used a property requirement that restricted political office to roughly the wealthiest 10-15% of the male citizen population, combined with co-optation of opposition leaders, publicly posted rewards for informants to sow distrust among conspirators, and heavy regulation of movement in central public spaces to prevent protest from spiraling into revolution.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: A reusable playbook for how unpopular minority regimes engineer institutional durability — co-optation, informant incentives, and public-space control — applicable as an analytical lens beyond ancient Greece.
insights.quote: perhaps the wealthiest 10–15% of the male citizen population, had access to political office
insights.locator: Ancient Greek oligarchies are a case study

insights.claim: When domestic conditions grew turbulent, international allies — Sparta in particular — routinely intervened militarily to restore 'order' and shore up allied oligarchic regimes elsewhere in Greece.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Identifies external military backing as a critical (and historically recurring) mechanism sustaining unpopular minority regimes, a pattern with modern analogues in great-power support for allied elites.
insights.quote: the Spartans in particular were notorious for intervening to help shore up oligarchies
insights.locator: To maintain power, the oligarchic 'gentlemen's club'

insights.claim: Contrary to the common narrative — promoted partly by the framers of the US Constitution and built on Thucydides' account of Athens — that ancient direct democracies inevitably collapsed into demagoguery and disorder, contemporary scholarship increasingly holds that between the third and second centuries BCE democracy may have been the most common form of government in the Greek world.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Undercuts a foundational premise used by US constitutional framers to justify anti-majoritarian design — that ancient democracy was inherently unstable — with newer archaeological and inscriptional evidence.
insights.quote: democracy may have been the most common form of government in the ancient Greek world
insights.locator: But contemporary scholars, drawing on new literary, inscriptional

insights.claim: Despite oligarchy's ubiquity in Greek antiquity, it had almost no positive public representation in art or culture — Critias' disputed tombstone would represent the sole known artistic depiction of oligarchia.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Shows oligarchy's chronic public-legitimacy deficit even at its historical peak — a pattern Simonton implies recurs for modern oligarchic power, which also avoids overt public self-identification.
insights.quote: Critias’ tombstone, if it existed, would represent the sole attested instance of oligarchia depicted in art
insights.locator: Then as now, hardly anyone spoke well of oligarchy in public

themes.theme: The framers, the filibuster, and American oligarchy

insights.claim: The US Constitution's framers shifted political discourse away from a 'democracy vs. oligarchy' framing and toward a contrast between the rule of law and the 'tyranny of the majority,' sidelining oligarchy as a category in American constitutional debate despite its centrality to the Greek political tradition the US drew on.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Explains why 'oligarchy' as a category is largely absent from mainstream American constitutional discourse today — a rhetorical framing choice made deliberately by the framers, per Simonton, rather than an accident.
insights.quote: the US Constitution’s framers shifted the terms of discourse away from “democracy vs. oligarchy” and toward a contrast between the rule of law and the tyranny of the majority
insights.locator: In many ways, American society is ill-prepared for this moment

insights.claim: Alexander Hamilton warned in Federalist No. 1 that 'a dangerous ambition more often lurks behind the specious mask of zeal for the rights of the people than under the forbidden appearance of zeal for the firmness and efficiency of government,' arguing that excess democracy, not its deficit, would cause instability.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Documents the framers' explicit wariness of majoritarian excess as the intellectual origin of the US system's anti-majoritarian features that Simonton later argues enable modern oligarchy.
insights.quote: a dangerous ambition more often lurks behind the specious mask of zeal for the rights of the people
insights.locator: But in the first of the Federalist Papers

insights.claim: James Madison clarified in Federalist 10 and 14 that 'republican' government in the US design primarily means representative rather than direct voting on legislation, with vote-tallying in both cases remaining majoritarian — undercutting the modern claim that the US was designed to be a 'republic, not a democracy' where majority votes need not prevail.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Directly rebuts a common modern political talking point ('republic, not a democracy') using the framers' own words, relevant to any commentary or debate on US majoritarian legitimacy.
insights.quote: what “republican” means in practice is primarily the use of representative, rather than direct, voting on legislation
insights.locator: As Madison explained in Federalist 10 and 14

insights.claim: Hamilton explicitly condemned supermajority voting rules in Federalist No. 22, writing that giving a minority a veto over the majority tends 'to substitute the pleasure, caprice, or artifices of an insignificant, turbulent, or corrupt junto, to the regular deliberations and decisions of a respectable majority' — language Simonton argues describes exactly how the modern US Senate filibuster now operates.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: The essay's central rhetorical pivot: uses the framers' own anti-supermajority arguments against the modern institution (the filibuster) that they would supposedly have condemned — a sharp framing for any debate on filibuster reform.
insights.quote: to substitute the pleasure, caprice, or artifices of an insignificant, turbulent, or corrupt junto, to the regular deliberations and decisions of a respectable majority
insights.locator: Hamilton, while not necessarily a friend of democracy

insights.claim: The US Senate filibuster lets two-fifths of senators, potentially representing only about 10% of the total US population, block legislation via a de facto veto, which Simonton argues means the US 'is effectively governed by an oligarchy.'
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: The article's core actionable claim for political/policy watchers: the filibuster is framed as the specific institutional mechanism by which a small population share currently holds effective veto power over US federal legislation.
insights.quote: America is effectively governed by an oligarchy, against the express intentions of the Federalists
insights.locator: Two-fifths of the Senate, potentially representing only about 10%

insights.claim: The term 'oligarchy' returned to prominent US political discourse via the 2010s Occupy Wall Street movement and Bernie Sanders's 2016 presidential campaign, then gained wider currency when Joe Biden warned in his January 2025 farewell address of 'an oligarchy... of extreme wealth, power, and influence that literally threatens our entire democracy.'
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Traces the recent political lineage of 'oligarchy' rhetoric in the US, from left-populist movements to a sitting president's farewell warning — context for reading current political discourse on wealth concentration.
insights.quote: an oligarchy … of extreme wealth, power, and influence that literally threatens our entire democracy

insights.claim: Simonton suggests modern American 'oligarchs' are now more likely imagined as tech billionaires than 1990s Russian oligarchs, drawing an analogy to the group of billionaires — Mark Zuckerberg, Jeff Bezos, Tim Cook, Sergey Brin, and Elon Musk — who flanked Donald Trump at his second inauguration, while questioning whether such rivalrous figures could actually cooperate long enough to sustain a stable ruling coalition, unlike ancient oligarchs who had institutions forcing cooperation.
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Names the specific individuals now popularly cast as an American 'oligarchy' and flags an open question — whether tech-billionaire rivalries prevent durable coordination the way ancient oligarchic institutions enforced it — relevant to any thesis about tech-mogul political coordination risk.
insights.quote: it is far from clear that they could cooperate long enough to sustain it
insights.locator: just imagine a government composed exclusively of the billionaires

insights.claim: Simonton frames unresolved US challenges — Trump's attacks on the rule of law, AI, climate change, and runaway inequality — as requiring defense of democratic-majority rights, warning that maintaining the status quo of supermajority veto points 'does the oligarchs' work for them.'
insights.speaker: Matt Simonton
insights.so_what: Links the essay's historical argument to a live policy agenda (AI, climate, inequality), framing institutional reform of majoritarian veto points as a precondition for progress on those issues.
insights.quote: To maintain the status quo is to do the oligarchs’ work for them
